name: Coalition Amplification
consequences: Immediate: Expand stakeholder coalition to include at least 10 civil society and industry partners → Systemic: 25% increase in policy influence score as measured by number of formal endorsements in procurement documents → Strategic: This balances the tension between political inertia and technical credibility, but may slow decision timelines due to coordination overhead.
options: Form a formal working group with existing privacy NGOs and academic institutions to co‑author policy briefs.
Launch a public consultation campaign inviting feedback from small businesses and citizen groups.
Create a cross‑sector consortium that offers joint funding and shared expertise, leveraging blockchain‑based membership verification to ensure transparency.
review: Controls Political Inertia vs. Technical Credibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost of sustained engagement and potential dilution of messaging.

name: Policy Framing
consequences: Immediate: Reframe platform dependency as a national security risk in official white papers → Systemic: 15% rise in budget allocation for digital sovereignty initiatives as tracked by parliamentary spending reports → Strategic: This shifts the tension between cost containment and strategic autonomy, but may trigger pushback from incumbent vendors.
options: Publish a comparative risk assessment highlighting supplier concentration impacts on service continuity.
Develop a narrative that links platform neutrality to resilience against geopolitical disruptions.
Introduce a legislative proposal that mandates platform‑neutral fallback paths in all future digital identity procurements, using a smart contract to enforce compliance.
review: Controls Cost Containment vs. Strategic Autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider the legal feasibility of mandating fallback paths.

name: Demonstrator Credibility
consequences: Immediate: Build a mobile demonstrator using GrapheneOS and FIDO2/WebAuthn → Systemic: 20% increase in stakeholder confidence measured by survey response rates → Strategic: This balances the tension between rapid prototyping and rigorous security validation, but may limit scalability.
options: Develop a lightweight mobile app that authenticates against a simulated identity provider using open standards.
Integrate a hardware token fallback that demonstrates browser‑based authentication without OS trust anchors.
Deploy a decentralized identity hub powered by blockchain to showcase a fully platform‑neutral authentication flow.
review: Controls Rapid Prototyping vs. Rigorous Security Validation. Weakness: The options fail to consider the operational complexity of maintaining a blockchain infrastructure.

name: Procurement Language
consequences: Immediate: Draft procurement clauses requiring platform‑neutral fallback → Systemic: 30% increase in procurement documents incorporating fallback language as measured by contract analysis → Strategic: This addresses the tension between vendor flexibility and national resilience, but may face legal challenges from suppliers.
options: Propose a clause that allows alternative authentication methods beyond Apple/Google APIs.
Introduce a requirement for open‑source verification of platform integrity signals.
Mandate a blockchain‑based audit trail for all authentication events to enforce fallback compliance.
review: Controls Vendor Flexibility vs. National Resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact on existing vendor contracts.

name: Standards Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Align fallback design with eIDAS2 and NSIS standards → Systemic: 10% improvement in cross‑border interoperability scores → Strategic: This balances the tension between local autonomy and EU harmonization, but may delay domestic deployment.
options: Map fallback requirements to existing eIDAS2 specifications and publish a compliance checklist.
Advocate for inclusion of fallback mechanisms in the upcoming EU Digital Identity Wallet framework.
Develop a proprietary standard that extends eIDAS2 with blockchain‑based verifiable credentials for fallback authentication.
review: Controls Local Autonomy vs. EU Harmonization. Weakness: The options fail to consider the time required for standard ratification.

name: Risk Communication
consequences: Immediate: Publish a risk report quantifying platform dependency impacts → Systemic: 40% increase in media coverage on digital sovereignty issues → Strategic: This shifts the tension between public awareness and stakeholder fatigue, but may expose the project to political backlash.
options: Release a data‑driven white paper highlighting the cost of potential platform outages.
Host a series of webinars featuring experts to discuss resilience scenarios.
Create an interactive online dashboard that visualizes dependency metrics using real‑time data feeds.
review: Controls Public Awareness vs. Stakeholder Fatigue. Weakness: The options fail to consider the resource intensity of continuous media engagement.

name: Fallback Pathway Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Deploy a modular fallback authentication module that operates independently of Apple/Google integrity APIs → Systemic: Reduces platform dependency by 70% of authentication flows, lowering supplier concentration risk by 70% as measured by critical path reliance → Strategic: This accelerates resilience but increases integration complexity, balancing speed of deployment against system complexity.
options: Implement a lightweight, open‑source fallback module that leverages FIDO2/WebAuthn on GrapheneOS, ensuring 30% of authentication flows remain platform‑neutral.
Extend the fallback module to support hardware‑token authentication via USB‑C and NFC, increasing platform‑neutral coverage to 60% while adding moderate integration overhead.

Integrate a cloud‑based fallback service using zero‑trust identity federation, achieving 90% platform‑neutral coverage and enabling rapid scaling, but requiring significant investment in secure infrastructure.
review: Controls Supplier Concentration vs. Integration Complexity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost of maintaining secure cloud infrastructure.

name: Platform‑Neutral Procurement Mandate
consequences: Immediate: Embed platform‑neutral clauses in AltID procurement documents → Systemic: Increases procurement compliance rate by 50% and reduces vendor lock‑in cost by 25% over five years → Strategic: This enhances digital sovereignty but may delay procurement cycles, balancing speed of acquisition against long‑term autonomy.
options: Add a mandatory platform‑neutral requirement to the next AltID tender, ensuring at least 30% of authentication services are vendor‑agnostic.
Introduce a phased procurement approach that allows initial vendor‑specific solutions with a 12‑month transition to platform‑neutral alternatives.

Mandate a dual‑vendor strategy that requires all future procurements to include both a platform‑neutral core and a platform‑specific extension, achieving 80% autonomy but extending contract timelines.
review: Controls Procurement Speed vs. Digital Sovereignty. Weakness: The options ignore potential resistance from incumbent vendors.

name: Resilience‑Centric Standards Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Propose resilience metrics to EU Digital Identity Wallet standards → Systemic: Elevates Denmark’s standard compliance score by 15% and reduces continuity risk by 40% as measured by incident response time → Strategic: This positions Denmark as a resilience leader but may divert resources from domestic implementation, balancing international leadership against local capacity.
options: Submit a resilience‑focused addendum to the EU Digital Identity Wallet specifications, targeting a 15% improvement in compliance metrics.
Collaborate with EU working groups to embed a fallback authentication requirement, raising Denmark’s compliance score by 25% while reallocating 10% of project resources.

Lead a pan‑European initiative to standardize platform‑neutral fallback mechanisms, achieving a 35% compliance boost and establishing Denmark as a standards authority, but requiring a 20% increase in EU‑level engagement effort.
review: Controls International Leadership vs. Local Resource Allocation. Weakness: The options fail to account for the time lag in EU standard adoption.

name: Stakeholder‑Driven Continuity Planning
consequences: Immediate: Conduct a cross‑sector continuity workshop to map critical authentication dependencies → Systemic: Identifies 80% of high‑risk points and reduces potential downtime by 30% through contingency planning → Strategic: This strengthens resilience but may expose political sensitivities, balancing transparency against stakeholder trust.
options: Organize a quarterly workshop with civil‑society, academia, and public agencies to identify critical dependencies, covering 30% of high‑risk points.
Expand the workshop series to include private sector partners, mapping 60% of high‑risk points and creating a shared contingency framework.

Launch a national continuity task force that integrates all identified high‑risk points into a formal policy, covering 80% of risks and establishing a 30% reduction in projected downtime, but requiring significant political coordination.
review: Controls Transparency vs. Political Sensitivity. Weakness: The options overlook the need for secure data handling during workshops.

name: Digital Sovereignty Advocacy
consequences: Immediate: Publish a policy brief framing platform dependency as a sovereignty risk → Systemic: Increases public awareness by 50% and elevates policy support for platform neutrality by 20% as measured by parliamentary motions → Strategic: This builds political momentum but may polarize public opinion, balancing advocacy reach against societal cohesion.
options: Release a concise policy brief highlighting platform dependency risks, targeting a 20% rise in parliamentary support.
Develop a multimedia campaign that educates citizens on digital sovereignty, achieving a 35% increase in public awareness and a 25% boost in policy backing.

Create an interactive digital platform that allows citizens to simulate fallback scenarios, driving a 50% increase in public engagement and a 40% rise in policy endorsements, but demanding substantial development resources.
review: Controls Public Engagement vs. Societal Cohesion. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential backlash from incumbent platform providers.

name: Emergent Trust Anchor Innovation
consequences: Immediate: Pilot a decentralized trust anchor using blockchain‑based identity attestations → Systemic: Reduces reliance on platform integrity signals by 60% and cuts authentication latency by 15% as measured by average response time → Strategic: This introduces cutting‑edge resilience but may face regulatory uncertainty, balancing innovation speed against compliance risk.
options: Deploy a proof‑of‑concept blockchain identity attestation layer that replaces 30% of platform integrity checks.
Scale the blockchain layer to cover 60% of authentication flows, integrating with existing FIDO2 endpoints and reducing latency by 10%.
Implement a fully decentralized trust anchor that handles 90% of authentication, achieving a 15% latency improvement and enabling rapid global deployment, but requiring new regulatory approvals.
review: Controls Innovation Speed vs. Regulatory Compliance. Weakness: The options ignore the maturity of blockchain identity standards.

name: Supplier Concentration Mitigation
consequences: Immediate: Implement a diversified vendor assessment framework. Systemic: Reduces supplier concentration risk by 30% within 12 months, measured by vendor share distribution index. Strategic: This enhances resilience but may increase procurement complexity and cost.
options: Conduct a comprehensive vendor risk audit and publish findings to inform procurement decisions.
Introduce a multi‑vendor procurement policy that requires at least two alternative platform providers for critical services.
Deploy a decentralized identity ledger using permissioned blockchain to eliminate single‑vendor lock‑in.
review: Controls Speed vs. Resilience. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact on existing contractual obligations with current platform providers.

name: Resilience‑Centric Procurement
consequences: Immediate: Embed resilience criteria into procurement specifications. Systemic: Increases procurement cycle time by 15% but improves system uptime by 20% over 24 months. Strategic: This balances cost against long‑term service continuity.
options: Add a resilience scorecard to vendor evaluation criteria to prioritize continuity features.
Mandate that all new contracts include a fallback authentication clause and a continuity‑of‑service SLA.
Adopt a micro‑services procurement model that allows rapid re‑deployment across multiple platforms via container orchestration.
review: Controls Cost vs. Continuity. Weakness: The options fail to consider the learning curve for procurement staff in interpreting resilience metrics.

name: Fallback Authentication Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Design a fallback authentication flow using open standards. Systemic: Enables 90% of critical user journeys to remain functional during platform outages, measured by service availability logs. Strategic: This increases system robustness but may introduce additional user onboarding steps.
options: Prototype a browser‑based fallback using WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on a privacy‑respecting OS.
Develop a hardware‑token fallback that can be issued to high‑risk users.
Implement a zero‑trust identity federation that dynamically routes authentication through multiple identity providers.
review: Controls User Experience vs. Robustness. Weakness: The options fail to consider the cost of distributing and managing hardware tokens at scale.

name: Platform‑Neutral Integration
consequences: Immediate: Create an abstraction layer for platform‑independent API calls. Systemic: Reduces platform dependency by 40% within 18 months, measured by API call distribution across OS platforms. Strategic: This improves flexibility but may increase development effort and maintenance overhead.
options: Build a middleware SDK that normalizes platform APIs for authentication services.
Adopt a cross‑platform framework like Flutter with native modules for secure credential storage.
Deploy a server‑side identity broker that translates platform‑specific tokens into a unified identity token using AI‑based semantic mapping.
review: Controls Development Speed vs. Flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider the regulatory implications of storing user credentials on third‑party servers.

name: Ecosystem Resilience Blueprint
consequences: Immediate: Draft a resilience blueprint for the national identity ecosystem. Systemic: Increases stakeholder engagement by 25% as measured by participation in workshops. Strategic: This fosters collaboration but may delay policy adoption due to broader consensus requirements.
options: Publish a white paper outlining resilience best practices for digital identity stakeholders.
Organize a multi‑stakeholder resilience summit to co‑create a shared roadmap.
Launch a public‑private partnership incubator that accelerates resilience innovations through joint funding and rapid prototyping.
review: Controls Policy Momentum vs. Stakeholder Buy‑In. Weakness: The options fail to consider the time needed for legal harmonization across different public agencies.

name: Digital Sovereignty Enforcement
consequences: Immediate: Advocate for mandatory platform‑neutral clauses in all future digital identity procurement. Systemic: Increases the likelihood of platform‑neutral language adoption by 35% within 24 months, measured by policy adoption rate. Strategic: This strengthens sovereignty but may face political resistance and extended negotiation cycles.
options: Submit a formal policy brief to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen highlighting sovereignty risks.
Launch a targeted lobbying campaign targeting key parliamentary committees.
Create a coalition‑driven open‑source platform that demonstrates sovereign identity capabilities and pressures procurement decisions.
review: Controls Political Leverage vs. Implementation Speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider the potential backlash from incumbent platform providers.
